channel.title: NYT > World > Americas
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Trump Assembles a New Coalition to ‘Eradicate’ Cartels
item.description: At a gathering in Florida, the president asked the leaders of a dozen Latin American and Caribbean nations to help the U.S. military crush armed trafficking groups.
item.dc:creator: Annie Correal and Shawn McCreesh
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross/The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump with Latin American and Caribbean leaders at the summit on Saturday. U.S. officials said a new coalition would aggressively target cartels and organized crime across the region.

item.title: Two Toronto-Area Synagogues Are Struck by Gunfire
item.description: The episodes, which happened late Friday and early Saturday, came after a third synagogue was damaged by gunfire on Monday, officials said.
item.dc:creator: Mark Walker
item.media:credit: Mert Alper Dervis/Anadolu, via Getty Images
item.media:description: The Beth Avraham Yoseph of Toronto Synagogue was damaged by gunfire late on Friday night, according to the mayor of Vaughan, a suburb of Toronto.

item.title: Traveling Around the Turbulent World With Mark Carney
item.description: The prime minister visited India, Australia and Japan to sell Canada to foreign investors and call for middle powers to unite, as the United States and Israel attacked Iran.
item.dc:creator: Ian Austen
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Mark Carney let his guard down after a speech in Australia.

item.title: At Trump’s Summit in Miami, Bolivia Makes a Political U-turn Toward the U.S.
item.description: For the past two decades, Bolivia resisted U.S. influence. A rightward shift is reorienting the country’s president toward Washington.
item.dc:creator: Benjamin Swift and Max Bearak
item.media:description: Rodrigo Paz, the president of Bolivia, after his swearing-in ceremony in La Paz in November.

item.title: Here Is Mexico’s Plan to Keep the Country Safe During the World Cup
item.description: Officials announced a massive deployment of security forces ahead of the world’s largest sporting event after recent violence prompted safety concerns for the millions of fans expected to attend.
item.dc:creator: James Wagner and David Shortell
item.media:description: Banorte Stadium, also known as Azteca Stadium, the venue in Mexico City that will host the opening match of the 2026 World Cup.

item.title: As Trump Focuses on Cuba, DOJ Seeks Charges Against Its Leaders
item.description: The order could set the stage for a drive for regime change in Cuba, a goal President Trump has expressed.
item.dc:creator: Glenn Thrush, Alan Feuer, Luke Broadwater and Michael Crowley
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Jefferson Siegel for The New York Times
item.media:description: Jason A. Reding Quiñones, the United States attorney for the Southern District of Florida, is said to be looking to bring criminal charges against Cuba’s leaders.

item.title: From 1995: Grammy-Winning Singer Selena Killed in Shooting at Texas Motel
item.description: Known as the queen of Tejano music, she was beloved as an idol and a heartthrob on both sides of the Mexican border.
item.dc:creator: Sam Howe Verhovek
item.media:credit: Dave Einsel/Houston Chronicle, via Associated Press
item.media:description: Selena performing in Houston in 1993.

item.title: From 2007: Vilma Espín, Rebel and Wife of Raúl Castro, Dies at 77
item.description: An idealistic socialite who fought alongside Fidel and Raúl Castro in the mountains of Cuba, she was the country’s unofficial first lady for decades.
item.dc:creator: Anthony DePalma
item.media:description: Vilma Espín around 1959. She became a widely recognized symbol of the Castro family’s half-century grip on power.

item.title: From 1957: Gabriela Mistral, Poet, Is Dead; Won Nobel Prize for Literature
item.description: She was recognized in 1945 for three “Soñetos de la Muerte” (“Sonnets of Death”), which were first published in Chile in 1922.
item.dc:creator: Special to The New York Times
item.media:description: Gabriela Mistral, seated, in 1945, showing her Nobel Prize to the Swedish writer Hjalmar Gullberg, right.

item.title: Defense Without U.S. Help Is a Live Topic for Canada, Japan and Australia
item.description: The leaders of Japan and Canada are making a unified front on defense cooperation as President Trump raises the pressure over military spending.
item.dc:creator: Ian Austen
item.media:credit: Adrian Wyld/The Canadian Press, via Associated Press
item.media:description: Prime Minister Mark Carney of Canada arriving in Tokyo on Friday as part of a tour that also included Australia.

item.title: Jamil Jivani, a Friend of JD Vance, Channels Charlie Kirk in Canada
item.description: Jamil Jivani, a Conservative rookie member of Parliament, follows an American playbook to win over young men on college campuses.
item.dc:creator: Norimitsu Onishi
item.media:credit: Gavin John for The New York Times
item.media:description: Jamil Jivani, a conservative member of Parliament, talking with supporters after speaking at the University of Calgary last month.

item.title: U.S. Restarts Diplomatic Relations With Venezuela
item.description: Washington has rapidly warmed up ties with Venezuela — and applied major pressure on it — since capturing the country’s president, Nicolás Maduro, two months ago.
item.dc:creator: Max Bearak
item.media:credit: Adriana Loureiro Fernandez for The New York Times
item.media:description: A protest last month in Caracas, Venezuela’s capital, demanding freedom for political prisoners.

item.title: Not Just Oil: In Venezuela, U.S. Interior Secretary Pushes for Mining Access
item.description: Venezuela is home to large reserves of rare earths, gold and other valuable minerals that the Trump administration wants to exert more control over.
item.dc:creator: Max Bearak and Lisa Friedman
item.media:description: Interior Secretary Doug Burgum and Delcy Rodríguez, Venezuela’s new leader, in Caracas on Wednesday.

item.title: A Mexico City Design Gallery Hits the World Stage
item.description: AGO Projects, which specializes in contemporary work, recently turned heads by standing by an artist who had become a political lightning rod.
item.dc:creator: Ray Mark Rinaldi
item.category: TEFAF (The European Fine Art Fair Stichting)
item.media:credit: Greta Rico for The New York Times
item.media:description: Rudy Weissenberg, left, and Rodman Primack in their Mexico City design gallery, AGO Projects. Weissenberg and Primack, longtime personal and professional partners, opened AGO six years ago.

item.title: James Luckey-Lange Recounts Being Detained, Starved and Beaten in Venezuela
item.description: James Luckey-Lange, 28, wrote about kindness and shared humanity as he traveled. But he said he had been shackled, starved and beaten in Venezuela after being detained.
item.dc:creator: Annie Correal and Anatoly Kurmanaev
item.category: Goodbye Horses: The Many Lives of Q Lazzarus (Movie)
item.media:credit: Hannah Yoon for The New York Times
item.media:description: James Luckey-Lange was held by Venezuelan authorities for more than a month and was released after then President Nicolás Maduro was captured in January.

item.title: Carney Says Canada and Australia Can ‘Set the Agenda’ as World Faces ‘Crises’
item.description: The Canadian prime minister is touring the Asia-Pacific to rejuvenate alliances in the wake of the U.S. trade war and President Trump’s calls for Canada’s annexation.
item.dc:creator: Ian Austen
item.media:description: Prime Minister Mark Carney of Canada addressing Parliament in Canberra, Australia, on Thursday.

item.title: Why Ecuador Invited the U.S. Military to Help With Its Drug Gangs
item.description: Drug gangs have turned the South American country into one of the most dangerous in the region and the world’s leading exporter of cocaine.
item.dc:creator: Luis Ferré-Sadurní, Eric Schmitt and José María León Cabrera
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Daniele Volpe for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Ecuadorean Coast Guard patrolling in 2025. The U.S. military is now helping Ecuador plan a series of raids targeting the South American country’s violent drug gangs.

item.title: Feminist Scholars and Activists on the Past and Future of Feminism
item.description: Feminist scholars and activists, including Gloria Steinem and Leymah Gbowee, reflected on a movement that they said remains unfinished.
item.dc:creator: Tanya Mohn

item.title: With Fuel Running Out and Canadian Flights Suspended, Cuba’s Tourism Is Collapsing
item.description: The Trump administration’s decision to cut off foreign oil to the island is devastating its tourism industry, a key source of income for a government being pushed to the edge.
item.dc:creator: Frances Robles and Vjosa Isai
item.media:description: Tourists in an old American car used as a taxi last month in Havana.

item.title: Toronto Synagogue Is Damaged by Gunfire on Night of Jewish Holiday
item.description: The temple said 20 shots were fired at the building but no one was hurt.
item.dc:creator: Rylee Kirk

item.title: U.S. Takes Military Action in Ecuador Against ‘Terrorist Organizations’
item.description: U.S. Special Forces soldiers are advising and supporting Ecuadorean commandos on raids across the country against suspected drug shipment facilities and other drug-related sites.
item.dc:creator: Eric Schmitt and Luis Ferré-Sadurní
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:description: Members of the Ecuadorean Navy stand guard in the facilities of the Coast Guard Command at the Port of Manta, Ecuador, in 2025.

item.title: Carney Says That Canada’s Support for Iran Airstrikes Came ‘With Regret’
item.description: The prime minister criticized Israel and the U.S. for not consulting other nations or the U.N. before their attack on Iran.
item.dc:creator: Ian Austen
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Canada’s prime minister, Mark Carney, said Iran had persisted in its nuclear ambitions in defiance of years of diplomatic efforts, but he also criticized the United States and Israel.

item.title: British Columbia Moving to Permanent Daylight Saving Time, Changing Clocks for the Last Time Sunday
item.description: The Canadian province is moving permanently to daylight saving time, officials announced.
item.dc:creator: Aimee Ortiz
item.media:description: The Gastown Steam Clock marking the noon hour in Vancouver, British Columbia.

item.title: Ceramics, Once the Lifeblood of Mexican Artisans, Are Under Pressure
item.description: In the state of Michoacán, industrial agriculture and organized crime threaten a cottage industry.
item.dc:creator: Michael Snyder and César Rodríguez

item.title: Under Pressure from Trump, Cuban Leader Calls for ‘Urgent’ Economic Change
item.description: President Miguel Díaz-Canel said the private sector needed more autonomy, as the island confronts a U.S. oil blockade that has deepened a humanitarian crisis.
item.dc:creator: Luis Ferré-Sadurní and David C. Adams
item.media:description: President Miguel Díaz-Canel of Cuba, center, at a funeral in January for those killed in U.S. strikes on Venezuela.

item.title: As Maduro and Khamenei Learned, It’s Harder Than Ever for Leaders to Hide
item.description: A surge in sensors and cameras, combined with artificial intelligence, has transformed U.S. intelligence’s ability to locate foreign heads of state. Add to that an American president willing to capture or kill them.
item.dc:creator: David E. Sanger
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
Wiretapping and Other Eavesdropping Devices and Methods
item.media:description: Nicolás Maduro was captured by U.S. forces in January after American intelligence was able to locate him at an apartment inside a Caracas military base.

item.title: After Attacks in Iran, Cubans Worry They Might Be Trump’s Next Target
item.description: Many Cubans are wondering if the Trump administration plans to target their country’s Communist government next.
item.dc:creator: Ed Augustin, Frances Robles and David C. Adams
item.media:description: A line of cars waits outside a gas station in Havana last month. Cuba is suffering from a severe energy shortage largely brought on by a U.S. blockade of foreign oil shipments,

item.title: Carney Hails New Ties Between Canada and India, Setting Aside Rift Over Killing
item.description: Prime Minister Mark Carney focused on business during a meeting with his Indian counterpart, Narendra Modi, with little mention of India’s alleged role in a deadly shooting in 2023.
item.dc:creator: Ian Austen
item.media:description: Prime Minister Mark Carney of Canada, left, with the Indian leader, Narendra Modi, in New Delhi on Monday.
